perturbation.original_text: (a) “ ​ Affiliate ​ ” means a Person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the Person specified of a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) “ ​ Affiliate ​ ” means any entity associated with a Party, regardless of control or ownership.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition aligns with the standard legal definition of an affiliate, which requires control. The modified definition removes the requirement of control, potentially misclassifying entities as affiliates that would not be considered so under typical legal interpretations. This can impact liability and obligations under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Affiliate' beyond what is legally recognized under securities laws, specifically 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 which focuses on 'control'. This deviation could have implications for reporting and compliance obligations if either party is a publicly traded company.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Affiliate' by removing the requirement of 'control,' which is a key element in the legal definition, particularly under securities laws. This is supported by the Law Explanation and aligns with 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, which, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, defines terms related to filings under the Securities Exchange Act and considers 'control' for determining affiliate status. By omitting 'control,' the modified definition could misclassify entities as affiliates, potentially leading to non-compliance with reporting and other obligations under securities laws. The original definition aligns with the standard legal meaning of affiliate requiring control. The new definition does not require control, therefore, it goes against the standard definition.

perturbation.original_text: (b) “ ​ Confidential ​ Information ​ ” means all information, whether visual, written, electronic or oral, (including any information furnished prior to the execution of this Agreement) related to the products, services, technology, business, customers, working capital, affairs, assets, condition (financial or otherwise) or prospects of a Disclosing Party which is furnished by such Disclosing Party, or otherwise disclosed by such Disclosing Party, to a Receiving Party in connection with the consideration and evaluation of the Transaction (including, without limitation, samples, compounds and ingredients thereof, prototypes, formulae, technical know how, technical data, financial information, payment terms, performance data, product specifications and customer and/or supplier information) and all reports, analyses, compilations, data, studies and other materials (in whatever form maintained, whether documentary, computer storage or otherwise) prepared by the Receiving Party containing or reflecting such furnished information.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) “ ​ Confidential ​ Information ​ ” means any information that a Disclosing Party subjectively believes to be confidential, regardless of its nature or means of communication. The Receiving Party's belief as to the confidentiality of the information is irrelevant.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition provides a reasonable scope of confidential information. The modified definition introduces a subjective standard based solely on the Disclosing Party's belief, removing any objective criteria. This creates ambiguity and potentially covers information not typically considered confidential or protectable, such as publicly available information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the definition of a trade secret under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). The UTSA requires that information derive independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and be subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified definition eliminates these requirements, potentially including information that is publicly available and thus not a trade secret, creating a legal conflict. The act states that trade secrets must derive value from not being generally known, where this contract provides it can be 'any information'.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include anything the Disclosing Party subjectively believes to be confidential, removing objective criteria and potentially including publicly available information. The Law Explanation indicates that this contradicts the UTSA definition of a trade secret, which requires independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and efforts to maintain its secrecy. Since the changed text doesn't require the information to not be readily ascertainable, it potentially contradicts trade secret protection laws. The scraped snippets are unhelpful.

perturbation.original_text: (f) “ ​ Representatives ​ ” means all Persons acting on behalf of a specified Party or its Affiliates, including without limitation, its directors, officers, lenders, employees, agents, representatives, financial advisors, attorneys, accountants, consultants, reserve and valuation and other experts.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) “ ​ Representatives ​ ” means any individual the Party deems a representative, regardless of their relationship or authority.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition provides a clear list of individuals who would be covered under the definition. The modified definition is very broad and includes any individual the party deems a 'representative' regardless of their relation, including a neighbor, or a random person on the street. There is no specific direction as to what a 'representative' is, and it can be as subjective as the party wants.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the basic principles of agency law, specifically Restatement (Third) of Agency § 1.01, which requires that an agent act on behalf of and subject to the control of the principal. The modified definition allows a party to designate anyone as a 'representative' without any requirement of control or a principal-agent relationship, potentially creating liability for actions of individuals who are not actually agents under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified text's broad definition of 'Representatives' contradicts agency law, specifically the requirement for an agent to act under the principal's control. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal.' The changed text allows a party to designate anyone as a representative without the necessary principal-agent relationship. This means the 'representative' may not be subject to the principal's control, directly contradicting the snippet's requirement for agency.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Non-Solicitation. The Company acknowledges that Chief Enterprises has invested and will continue to invest significant time and money to develop valuable, continuing relationships with existing and prospective clients and customers of Chief Enterprises. The Company further recognizes that it will obtain valuable Confidential Information about Chief Enterprises customers, clients, suppliers, business partner, and/or vendors, and their business requirements. The Company agrees that, for the term of this Agreement and for two (2) years following its termination, Company will not directly or indirectly, solicit or assist others in soliciting, or attempt to solicit or assist others in attempting to solicit, (i) any existing customers, clients, suppliers, business partners, and/or vendors of Chief Enterprises with or about whom the Company obtained Confidential Information, or (ii) any prospective customers, clients, suppliers, business partners, and/or vendors of Chief Enterprises with or about whom the Company obtained Confidential Information and with whom Chief Enterprises took significant steps to do business with during the term of this Agreement, for the purpose or effect of inducing such existing or prospective customers, clients, suppliers, business partners, and/or vendors to cease doing business or reduce their business with Chief Enterprises or to purchase, lease, or utilize products or services that are competitive with, similar to, or that may be used as substitutes for any products or services offered by Chief Enterprises.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Non-Solicitation. The Company acknowledges that Chief Enterprises has invested and will continue to invest significant time and money to develop valuable, continuing relationships with existing and prospective clients and customers of Chief Enterprises. The Company further recognizes that it will obtain valuable Confidential Information about Chief Enterprises customers, clients, suppliers, business partner, and/or vendors, and their business requirements. The Company agrees that, for the term of this Agreement and perpetually following its termination, Company will not directly or indirectly, solicit or assist others in soliciting, or attempt to solicit or assist others in attempting to solicit, (i) any existing customers, clients, suppliers, business partners, and/or vendors of Chief Enterprises, or (ii) any prospective customers, clients, suppliers, business partners, and/or vendors of Chief Enterprises, for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause contains a 2-year limitation. The changed clause removes a 2-year limitation of the non-solicitation provision and expands the restriction to a perpetual term and removes that the company had to have gotten confidential information about the clients. This creates an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification likely violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A perpetual non-solicitation agreement is likely considered an unreasonable restraint of trade and is thus unenforceable in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by removing the 2-year limitation on the non-solicitation clause and making it perpetual. This violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." By making the non-solicitation agreement perpetual, the modified contract creates an unreasonable restraint of trade, directly contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Governing Law ​ . This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois applicable to agreements made and to be performed within such State and all disputes shall be venued exclusively in state or federal courts situated in Chicago, Illinois.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Governing Law ​ . This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Atlantis, and all disputes shall be resolved through trial by combat.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly establishes Illinois law as governing and specifies venue. The modified text specifies the Laws of Atlantis as governing. Atlantis is a fictional location. This is so vague that there is no place to execute the governing law, making it unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Contract Law - Requirement of Definite Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification renders the governing law clause unenforceable because the laws of Atlantis do not exist. Contract law requires definite and certain terms, including a real and applicable jurisdiction for governing law (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 33). Specifying a non-existent jurisdiction creates uncertainty and makes the contract difficult, if not impossible, to enforce.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Illinois law, which would be a real, enforceable legal system. The modified contract replaces this with 'the laws of Atlantis,' a fictional place. The Law Explanation states that contract law requires definite and certain terms, including a real and applicable jurisdiction for governing law. The Perturbation Explanation says this makes the governing law unenforceable. Because 'Atlantis' does not exist, there is no legal system to govern the contract, creating a direct contradiction.
